steps.title: Identify the lower bound of g(x)
steps.content:
We note that $2^{x}>0$ for all real $x$.
Multiplying by 6 and adding 7 gives $6\cdot2^{x}+7>7$.
Dividing by 11 yields $$ g(x) = \frac{1}{11}(6\cdot2^{x}+7) > \frac{7}{11}.$$
Thus $g(x)$ is always greater than $\frac{7}{11}$.

steps.title: Determine the greatest possible k
steps.content:
As $x\to -\infty$, $2^{x}\to 0^{+}$, so $$ g(x) \to \frac{1}{11}(6\cdot0+7) = \frac{7}{11}$$ from above.
Hence $g(x)$ can be made arbitrarily close to $\frac{7}{11}$ but never below it.
If $k > \frac{7}{11}$, choose $x$ sufficiently negative to make $g(x) \lt k$, violating the condition.
If $k = \frac{7}{11}$, then $g(x) > k$ for every $x$.
Therefore the greatest possible constant $k$ is $\frac{7}{11}$.
That matches choice C — C is correct.
